title: Methodology for the 2018 iwi affiliation estimated counts | Stats NZ
content:
## Methodology for the 2018 iwi affiliation estimated counts
22 June 2021, 10:45am
E ngā mana, e ngā reo, tēnei te reo mihi ki ngā pari kārangaranga o te motu, tēnā koutou katoa.
This paper provides information on the methodology used to produce the 2018 iwi affiliation estimates.
Strengths and limitations of 2018 iwi affiliation estimated counts provides an overview of the data and guidance to iwi data users on how to use the estimated counts.
In April 2019 Stats NZ announced that it would not release 2018 Census official statistical counts of iwi due to the number of census records missing iwi affiliation data (iwi data) and the lack of alternative government data sources to fill the gaps. Since then, Stats NZ and iwi data technicians from the Data Iwi Leaders Group (Data ILG) have looked at methods to reduce the impact of missing iwi data. This work has led to the release of the 2018 iwi affiliation estimated counts on Te Whata and Stats NZ website at Iwi affiliation (estimated counts): 2018 .
Background to iwi affiliation data Overview of the methods used Developing and testing the methods Iwi added to the classification in 2017 Producing estimates for iwi groupings Further information

## Background to iwi affiliation data
An iwi, or Māori tribe, is one of the largest kinship groupings and is generally made up of several hapū that are all descended from a common ancestor. Hapū are clusters of whānau, where the whānau is usually an extended family grouping consisting of children, parents, often grandparents, and other closely related kin. For statistical purposes, an iwi is defined as a whakapapa-based kinship grouping that generally has several hapū and one or more active marae, and a recognised structure that represents the interests of the iwi, such as a rōpū whakahaere, committee, or board.
Stats NZ releases iwi statistics based on the Iwi and iwi-related groups statistical classification . There are 175 iwi listed in the classification, including 43 'partially coded' iwi where a response has been received which is not able to be fully coded to a specific iwi (such as, categories within the group 'Confederations and waka, iwi not named'). The classification also includes the category 'Don’t know' and some residual categories to capture aspects such as 'Not stated' and 'Response unidentifiable'. Purpose of the Iwi Statistical Standard and Classification gives information on the classification.
When completing the 2018 Census questionnaire, respondents who indicated that they are of Māori descent or that they don’t know if they are of Māori descent were asked if they know the name(s) of their iwi. If they answered yes, they were then asked to specify up to five iwi and the region using free text fields. Respondents could specify 'Don’t know' as a valid response to the iwi question. As part of census processing, the text responses given by respondents for the iwi and region questions are coded to a large list of common 'synonyms'. These synonyms are then mapped to the iwi classification for output purposes.
There are several differences between the 2013 and 2018 Census questionnaires. Iwi (information about this variable and its quality) has a detailed summary of the differences.
These are the two significant changes to the online questionnaire in the 2018 Census.
- Only one name and one region textbox were initially displayed, and a respondent had to click a button labelled ‘add’ to add additional iwi. In the 2013 Census, five sets of textboxes were presented upfront.
- As-you-type functionality was added to help respondents provide valid, detailed responses. This functionality brings up common synonyms which match the text being entered by the user as they type.
The estimated iwi counts represent iwi for the usually resident Māori descent population in the 2018 Census. This is consistent with the population who was included in the 2013 Census iwi counts.
The level of response achieved in the 2018 Census was low, particularly for the Māori descent population. Several methods were used as part of the census processing to reduce the impact of this and improve the quality of census outputs. This included identifying people from administrative data sources who should be added to the census file (labelled Admin enumerated in figure 1), and looking to source census characteristics such as age, sex, and income from the 2013 Census or administrative data sources. 2018 Census: How we combined administrative data and census forms data to create the census dataset gives more information on the methods used.
An impact of the change in methodology used in the 2018 Census is that the usually resident Māori descent population counts increased by 30 percent compared with the 2013 Census counts, up from 668,721 to 869,850. This increase is higher than would be expected due to natural population change (births, deaths, and migration) and reflects improvements in the quality of Māori descent population counts in the 2018 Census. Māori descent (information about this variable and its quality) provides more information on the changes in the Māori descent population.
No alternative data sources were used where there was missing iwi data as part of census processing. The impact of this is that the 2018 Census was missing iwi data for 29 percent of the Māori descent population. For this reason iwi data was given a quality rating of ‘very poor’ and not released as 2018 Census official counts. Iwi (information about this variable and its quality) provides more information on the initial quality evaluation of the iwi variable in the 2018 Census.
Figure 1 compares the iwi data in the 2013 and 2018 Censuses (note that numbers are rounded to the nearest 1,000). It shows:
- people with Māori descent increased 30 percent between the 2013 Census (669,000) and 2018 Census (870,000)
- the number of people who either stated an iwi or indicated they don’t know their iwi is similar for the two census datasets, at 647,000 in the 2013 Census and 618,000 in the 2018 Census
- because of the previous two points, the proportion of those of Māori descent who stated an iwi or indicated they don’t know their iwi decreased from 97 percent in the 2013 Census to 71 percent in the 2018 Census
- the percentage of responses that were not stated or could not be coded was 3 percent (22,000) in the 2013 Census and 8 percent (68,000) in the 2018 Census
- for the 2018 Census there were 21 percent (184,000) of the Māori descent population added through admin enumeration and these individuals do not have iwi information.

## Overview of the methods used
Iwi counts produced from the 2018 Census would not be fit for use without adjustment as they would be too low and also under-represent groups more likely to be missed by the census.
To improve the quality of the iwi estimates we used three steps:
- Iwi data was sourced where possible for people missing iwi data from either the 2013 Census or from the iwi provided by the parents of children aged under 15 years.
- Additional iwi data was sourced from the 2013 Census or from the iwi provided by the parents of children aged under 15 for individuals where we had evidence that the design of the 2018 Census online form may have caused iwi to be under reported.
- A set of weights were developed to ‘rate-up’ the people we have iwi data for so that they represent the 2018 Census usually resident Māori descent population.
These three steps are covered in more detail in the following sections.
'People missing iwi data' is defined as the 2018 Census Māori descent population who do not have any non-residual iwi response. Residual categories include 'not stated' 'response outside of scope', 'refused to answer' and 'response unidentifiable'.
Table 1 provides the breakdown of final data sources used for the total number of iwi. Respondents identifying with more than one iwi are represented once for each iwi they identify with.
Data sources for 2018 iwi estimates
Data sources for 2018 iwi estimates
Total iwi affiliation count (000)

Note : Due to rounding, individual figures may not always sum to the stated total(s). Source : Stats NZ
Note : Due to rounding, individual figures may not always sum to the stated total(s).
This is the first time we have used this experimental methodology, which was developed in collaboration with Data ILG. We recognise that the use of alternative data sources and weighting are a significant change to the standard methods Stats NZ applies to census data, but without these adjustments, the iwi data from the 2018 Census would remain unusable. There is continuing work to address issues relating to Stats NZ’s social licence to use alternative data sources for census and wider statistical use purposes as identified by the 2018 External Data Quality Panel. Implementing the recommendations of the 2018 External Data Quality Panel provides updates on work Stats NZ is doing to address the recommendations.

### Step 1: Source iwi data from other sources
For people with missing iwi data we sourced iwi data from the 2013 Census and from parents of children aged under 15. The 2013 Census was used in the first instance, otherwise parental iwi data was used if available. We did not explore alternative administrative data sources due to the quality of this data. Identifying Māori populations using administrative data gives a review of administrative data sources of iwi data such as data collected by the Ministry of Education.

#### Using 2013 Census data
Using iwi data from the 2013 Census reduced the proportion of people missing iwi data from 29 percent to 17 percent.
In 2017 the iwi classification was reviewed and updated. This review included 32 iwi being added to the classification. To recode the 2013 iwi data to the new classification we took the synonyms coded for each individual in the 2013 Census and mapped these to the new classification. For example, in the 2013 Census Te Paatu Teretere was a synonym listed for 0102 Ngāti Kahu. If a respondent listed their iwi as Te Paatu Teretere in the 2013 Census, the response would have been coded to 0102 Ngāti Kahu. Following the 2017 classification change, Te Paatu Teretere is now listed under 0117 Te Paatu. Our process for recoding the 2013 Census responses would be to map the response to 0117 Te Paatu instead of 0102 Ngāti Kahu.
There are many reasons the iwi provided by a person in the 2013 Census may differ from the response they provided in the 2018 Census. These include changes in the questionnaire and collection mode, changes in the classification and coding practices, as well as changes in the iwi that an individual identifies with or chooses to report in the census.
To test the accuracy of using 2013 Census data, we compared the subset of people who responded and provided iwi affiliation in both censuses, linking together the 2018 Census and 2013 Census response for each person. The chance of a person reporting an iwi in 2018 that they reported in 2013 was 78 percent. The rates for most fully coded iwi lie between 56 and 83 percent, with larger iwi tending to have higher consistency.
An alternative to using the 2013 Census data directly would have been to use this data as input into a statistical process to impute or predict a likely iwi response in the 2018 Census. In consultation with Data ILG we decided that directly using the response an individual provided was more appropriate than statistical imputation. As such, we have not used statistical methods to impute responses in the production of 2018 iwi affiliation estimates.

#### Using parental iwi data for children
For children aged under 15 years who were still missing iwi data, we looked to use the iwi reported by their parents. The union of the iwi reported by both parents was used (for example, if one parent reported Ngāpuhi and the other parent reported Raukawa and Ngāti Hei, then the iwi listed for the child would be Ngāpuhi, Raukawa, and Ngāti Hei). This step sourced iwi data for around 10,000 children and reduced the proportion of people missing iwi data to 16 percent. While small in comparison to making use of 2013 Census data, this step helps to reduce an important gap in the iwi data, particularly for children aged under five who were born after the 2013 Census.
Based on genealogical connection, we might reasonably expect tamariki (children) to have the same iwi affiliations as their parent/s. There are a range of reasons why this might not occur in the census. First, in the census we are not able to distinguish between biological, whāngai, adopted, and stepchildren. Second, adults may complete the forms of children less thoroughly than their own form, or they may not know all of the iwi that the other parent affiliates with.
To understand this, we looked at 2018 Census records where a valid iwi response was provided for children and their parent(s). We found the iwi reported for a child exactly matched all the iwi reported for least one parent half the time, and matched or was a subset of the iwi reported for at least one parent 95 percent of the time.
The match rate between a child and both parents was significantly lower. Because of this we considered an approach where the iwi from a single parent was used. However, the union of the parents was used as this aligned more closely to the definition of the variable we are looking to measure. This point also relates to step 2 where additional iwi data is sourced from the parents of a child.

### Step 2: Additional imputation to reduce mode effect
The proportion of respondents completing the census online has increased from very few in 2006, to one-in-three in 2013, to the majority in 2018. Additionally, the design of the online collection platform changed over this period. In 2006 and 2013, the online form was designed to mimic the paper questionnaire. In 2018, it was designed to best make use of online functionality to improve respondent experience (for example, as-you-type functionality) and maximise the quality of data collected.
The average number of iwi for people who reported at least one iwi reduced from 1.58 in the 2013 Census to 1.44 in the 2018 Census. It is likely that the reduction was caused by a change in the design of the online form. In the 2013 Census the online form mirrored the paper form and presented the respondent with five iwi fields. In the 2018 Census the respondent was presented with a single iwi field with an ‘add more’ button for adding additional iwi.
To assess whether the reduction may have been caused by the change in the design we looked at the change in the total number of iwi responses by response mode. We restricted this assessment to people who responded with a non-residual iwi response in both censuses. This showed that:
- for individuals who responded via paper to both censuses, 0.5 percent fewer total iwi responses were received in 2018
- for individuals who responded online to both censuses, 6 percent fewer total iwi responses were received in 2018
- for individuals who responded in 2013 via paper and in 2018 online, 12 percent fewer total iwi responses were received in 2018.
There is strong evidence that the change in the design of the iwi question in the online form has reduced the number of iwi being reported by respondents. To reduce the impact of the change in the design we sourced additional iwi data from the 2013 Census for respondents who:
- responded to both the 2013 Census and 2018 Census
- reported one iwi in the 2018 Census and multiple iwi in the 2013 Census
- reported iwi in the 2018 Census that matched one of the iwi reported in the 2013 Census.
In this situation the additional iwi listed by an individual in the 2013 Census were added to the 2018 Census response. This did not affect the number of people for whom we had iwi data but this increased the number of iwi reported by around 43,000 (4 percent).
A similar approach was applied for children aged under 15. If one iwi was reported for a child, the parents of the child reported more than one iwi, and the iwi reported for the child matched at least one iwi reported by the parents, then the parents’ additional iwi were added to the child’s iwi data. This step increased the number of iwi by around 14,000 (1.3 percent).

### Step 3: Create non-response weights
Steps 1 and 2 reduced the proportion of missing iwi data for the usually resident Māori descent population from 29 percent to 16 percent. To account for the remaining people we were missing iwi data for, we developed a set of non-response weights. Under this approach a weight is attached to each person that we have iwi data for. The sum of the weights across these individuals is designed to represent the 2018 Census usually resident Māori descent population and to account for how the rate of missing iwi data varies across different groups. Population estimates for iwi can be formed by summing the weights across people with iwi data across the characteristic of interest.
The purpose of the weights is both to increase total iwi affiliation counts, and to reduce the potential of the estimates being biased due to the people responding to the census having different characteristics to the people who did not provide iwi data. In particular, we are looking to capture the variation in census participation rates across different areas in New Zealand as this may impact the estimated population counts for each iwi.
We developed the weights by specifying a model which estimates the probability that a person with certain characteristics would provide iwi data, and then calculating the weight as the inverse of that probability. For example, we may estimate that the chance that a 20-year-old male in Northland has iwi data is 50 percent. In this case, 20-year-old males in Northland that do have iwi data will be given a weight of two.
To model the probability that a person was missing iwi data we used logistic regression with person effects of single year age groups by sex and total personal income groupings, and area level effects for regional council and statistical area level 2 (SA2). Contact details are provided at the end of this page if further explanation of the equation is required.
We modelled the probability of a person i having a valid iwi response as
- X are person-level characteristics from the census and included effects for single year age groups by sex, and total personal income groupings. The effects of these variables were assigned independent prior distributions of a student t distribution with mean of zero, 3 degrees of freedom, and a scale of 4. No shrinkage occurs across these groups of parameters and they can be viewed as 'fixed effects'.
- r is the area level effect for regional council r.
- is the area level effect for statistical area level 2 (SA2) s.
- The parameters and are given prior distributions from a student t distribution with mean of zero, 3 degrees of freedom, and scale of 10 which is restricted to positive numbers.
The model was fitted in a Bayesian framework and implemented using the brms package in R, which makes use of the statistical programming language Stan. Stan makes use of Markov chain Monte Carlo (MCMC) methods to approximate the posterior distribution. To ease computation, we fitted the model on a random sample of 100,000 records. We used four chains in the model fitting, with 10,000 iterations for each chain, and half of the sample used for burn-in. This resulted in 20,000 simulations from the posterior distribution for inference purposes which were thinned to 1,000. All of the model parameters had Gelman-Rubin diagnostic (R̂) of less than 1.02 indicating that it is likely that the simulation had converged to the posterior distribution by the end of the burn-in period.
A range of other models were explored. These included using a spline to model the age and sex effects and included additional person-level variables such as highest qualification, labour force status, and household income. Further work could have been done to understand the benefit of using more structured priors for the area level effects. However, the resulting model seemed to perform well so this work was not pursued further.
The key aspect in the model for dealing with some iwi being over or under-represented in the data are the area level variables. We assume that if we account for the different rates of response by SA2 and regional council, then we have accounted for the under- and over-representation that might occur with people from a given iwi being more or less likely to report iwi data (after trying the first step of using 2013 Census and parental iwi data where we can). This approach is similar to a non-response adjustment commonly used in household surveys, except instead of forming K separate non-response cells and calculating adjustment factors within each of those, we fit a model with regional council effects and SA2 level effects. For SA2s with a small number of Māori, the SA2 effect will be ‘shrunk’ close to zero and the effect will then be the regional council level effect. Fitting the model in this way means we avoid the process of hand crafting the non-response cells to ensure the number of Māori in each cell is sufficient to estimate the adjustment factor adequately. It also allows some person level characteristics to be included in the model which would be difficult if forming mutually exclusive non-response cells.
The person level characteristics age, sex, and income are included in the model to improve the quality of estimates for characteristics of iwi. This is particularly important for very young children and young adults. We don’t have a wide range of variables in the model. We assume that if we account for the variation in response by geography, and variation in income, age, and sex (fit at the national level), then this will account for variation in response by different types of people within areas. These assumptions may not always hold, which is why we use 2013 Census and parental iwi data to reduce the level of missing data where possible.

## Developing and testing the methods
Our process for developing and testing the methodology involved two stages: an initial deep dive, followed by a wider review.
In the deep dive review we focused on five iwi and our goal was to build our understanding of the issues and develop an estimation approach. Alongside this methodology, which we will refer to as the standard approach, two alternative estimators were also developed.
- The first was an 'expectation' based on 2013 Census data. The expectation was developed by recoding the 2013 Census responses to the new classification, and then inflating the count to adjust for the growth in the underlying Māori descent population.
- The second was a 'weighting only' approach. This approach omitted step 1 and relied entirely on the weighting to account for people with no iwi data. We used this approach to help identify areas where the use of 2013 Census data may not be appropriate. This approach retained step 2, where additional iwi are sourced for people containing a single iwi, as an alternative approach for accounting for the impact of the change in the form that was not developed.
As part of this review we compared the population counts by geography for the three estimators, and across a range of personal and household characteristics. From this process we found the methods developed to reduce the impact of non-response seemed to be performing well, and we wanted to assess how they would perform across a wider number of iwi.
In the wider review our goal was to identify iwi where there may be quality concerns either with the methodology or the underlying data that required further investigation. In this review we focused on three aspects: consistency between the three estimators for the iwi population total, the level of response, and any coding issues noted during census processing. During this review we identified that the approach was not working well for iwi added to the classification in 2017. We also identified a number of iwi where coding issues were having a meaningful impact on their counts and these have been noted in Iwi affiliation (estimated counts): 2018 data sources and quality by iwi .

## Iwi added to the classification in 2017
While for most iwi the two estimates based on 2018 Census data were very similar, for iwi added to the classification in 2017 the approach of using 2013 Census data tended to be between 2 percent and 10 percent lower than the weighting only approach. This indicated that even though we had recoded the 2013 Census data to the new classification, making use of the 2013 Census responses was causing a downward bias for some iwi. We considered using the weighting only methodology for all iwi added to the classification in 2017. However, for many iwi the population estimates from the two approaches were very similar, and in these cases our preference was to use the standard approach as it enables a greater number of the Māori descent population to directly contribute to the iwi estimates and reduces the potential for bias in variables not included in the non-response model. There is not a definitive point where the weighting only approach is more appropriate than the standard approach. For iwi where the contribution from 2013 Census data was less than 5 percent and the difference between the iwi total for the two estimators was greater than 10 percent we used the weighting only approach. For these iwi, the value added by being able to include additional people from the 2013 Census in the estimation was low, and there was a high risk that the approach of using 2013 Census data would lead to the population total being under-estimated by a significant amount. Iwi where the weighting only estimation methodology was used are listed below. In the majority of these cases none of the synonyms associated with that iwi were observed in 2013 Census responses.
- 0109 Ngāti Whātua (not Ōrākei or Kaipara)
- 0113 Ngāti Whātua o Kaipara
- 0114 Ngāti Whātua o Ōrākei
- 0713 Maungaharuru Tangitū
- 0716 Te Hika o Pāpāuma
- 0905 Ngāti Whitikaupeka (Rangitīkei)
- 0906 Ngāi Te Ohuake (Rangitīkei)
- 0907 Ngāti Tamakōpiri (Rangitīkei)
- 0910 Tamahaki (Ruapehu, Waimarino).
All of the above are iwi added to the classification in 2017, with the exception of 0109 Ngāti Whātua (not Ōrākei or Kaipara). Prior to the 2017 review 0109 Ngāti Whātua (not Ōrākei or Kaipara) was named 0109 Ngāti Whātua and included responses now coded to 0113 Ngāti Whātua o Kaipara and 0114 Ngāti Whātua o Ōrākei. The weighting only approach was used for all three iwi to ensure a consistent approach was used for this group of the population.

## Producing estimates for iwi groupings
A common use of iwi data is to produce statistics for iwi groupings (known as iwi collectives on Te Whata) which include a number of iwi. The Census iwi grouping recode is an example of this. Under this approach the total is not the sum of the totals for each iwi, but the number of people who identify with at least one iwi in the group. This is because a person may affiliate with two iwi that are within the same iwi grouping.
For producing estimates for iwi groups the standard estimation methodology should be used rather than the ‘weighting only’ approach. The exception to this is if forming a grouping of the three Ngāti Whātua iwi – for this grouping the weighting only approach should be used as the estimates for all three iwi in the group use that estimation approach.
The census iwi grouping recode includes a grouping ‘other’ which includes the residual iwi categories (such as ‘response outside scope’). The contribution of the residual iwi codes will only contribute to counts for this iwi grouping if a respondent provided a residual response alongside a non-residual iwi response (for example, they provided an iwi response of 'Te Paatu' as well as an out-of-scope response such as ‘Samoan’). In other situations, residual responses have either been replaced by sourcing alternative iwi data or represented in the weighted responses of people providing non-residual iwi responses.
The 2018 iwi estimates are now available on Te Whata and from Iwi affiliation (estimated counts): 2018 .
Strengths and limitations of 2018 iwi affiliation estimated counts provides an overview of the data and guidance to iwi data users on how to use the estimated counts.
2018 iwi affiliation estimated counts – Datainfo+ provides detailed information about the quality of the estimates to help users make an informed decision on whether the estimates will be fit for their purpose.
Iwi affiliation (estimated counts): 2018 data sources and quality by iwi provides key quality information for each iwi.
